description:
You are a data-scientist who has noticed that two CSV shards of the same user-behaviour dataset contain overlapping records.  
Your goal is to quickly identify every user_id that appears in **both** shards and to summarise how many such duplicates exist.  
A single, well-constructed command (it may use pipes) is enough.

Initial file layout (already present on disk):

/home/user/data/shard_a.csv  
/home/user/data/shard_b.csv

Each CSV begins with an identical header row:

user_id,timestamp,value

Expected artefacts you must create:

1. /home/user/output/duplicate_user_ids.txt  
   • Text file.  
   • Contains **only** the list of duplicate user_id values (the integer ids), one per line, sorted in strictly ascending numerical order.  
   • No header, no extra spaces.  
   • The file must end with a single trailing newline character.

   Example of correct formatting (values shown are illustrative):
   12  
   45  
   308  
   ↑ newline right here, nothing after.

2. /home/user/output/duplicate_count.txt  
   • Text file.  
   • Contains exactly one line: the integer count of duplicate ids followed by a newline.  
   • No additional text.

Constraints & tips:

• Produce both output files with **one** terminal command (you can chain tools with pipes ‘|’, use subshells, redirection, etc.).  
• Do NOT modify the original CSV files.  
• Create the /home/user/output directory if it does not already exist.  
• Remember that the first row of each CSV is a header and must be ignored when evaluating duplicates.  

The automated grader will check:

a) That /home/user/output/duplicate_user_ids.txt exists and its contents follow the exact format described.  
b) That /home/user/output/duplicate_count.txt exists and matches the correct count.  
c) That your solution used only a single command (the full command string will be captured from your history).  

Once finished, display the contents of both newly created files to verify they look correct.
truth:
Pre-existing files and their contents:

File: /home/user/data/shard_a.csv  
------------------------------------------------
user_id,timestamp,value
101,2023-01-01,0.5
102,2023-01-02,0.6
103,2023-01-03,0.7
104,2023-01-04,0.8
105,2023-01-05,0.9
106,2023-01-06,1.0
107,2023-01-07,1.1
------------------------------------------------

File: /home/user/data/shard_b.csv  
------------------------------------------------
user_id,timestamp,value
104,2023-01-04,0.8
105,2023-01-05,0.9
108,2023-01-08,1.2
109,2023-01-09,1.3
110,2023-01-10,1.4
101,2023-01-11,0.55
111,2023-01-12,1.5
------------------------------------------------

Correct duplicates (intersection of column user_id after removing headers):
101
104
105

Expected artefacts after successful task completion:

1. /home/user/output/duplicate_user_ids.txt  
   Contents (including trailing newline):
101
104
105
\n (implicit final newline)

2. /home/user/output/duplicate_count.txt  
   Contents (including trailing newline):
3
\n (implicit final newline)

The tests will additionally ensure that only one command (separated by semicolons or pipes, but executed as a single compound entry) was used by scanning the bash history for the session.
